access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for CME00144

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00144
notes: The pinned file is 508,311 bytes.
Its direct poem division contains 186 direct LG elements and 2,232 direct L elements; 42 further LG and 301 further L elements occur inside editorial NOTE apparatus.

rights.basis: The CME repository contains an Unlicense file, but its scope over imported U-M transcription and encoding content is not explicit enough to infer unrestricted downstream rights for every layer.
rights.component: Checked-in XML file as a combined transcription, markup, and repository artifact

access.entityLabel: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse digital text CME00144

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse digital text CME00144
notes: U-M describes the CME texts as manually keyed from public-domain editions and proofread in house, with TEI structural markup.
The public item identifies Kaluza 1890 as the source edition.

rights.basis: The item displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright - United States statement; this does not override site terms for unrelated components.
rights.component: U-M digital text and presentation

bibliographic.citation: Max Kaluza, Libeaus Desconus: Die mittelenglische Romanze vom Schönen unbekannten, nach sechs Handschriften kritisch herausgegeben (Leipzig: O. R. Reisland, 1890).
bibliographic.title: Libeaus Desconus: Die mittelenglische Romanze vom Schönen unbekannten

entities.label: Max Kaluza's Libeaus Desconus (1890)
notes: A reconstructive critical edition based on six witnesses, not a diplomatic transcript of any one manuscript.
The printed reading text has 2,232 numbered lines in 186 twelve-line stanzas, with extensive variant passages and commentary in notes.

rights.basis: First published in 1890 and in the public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1890 edition text, notes, and editorial matter

entities.label: August Rhode's working copies of the Lincoln's Inn, Ashmole, Lambeth, and Cotton texts
notes: Kaluza says Rhode had prepared copies of I, A, L, and C before Kaluza checked them throughout against the manuscripts.
The current schema has no working-transcript entity type; manuscript_witness is used provisionally for this material intermediary, not to claim medieval date or independent textual authority.
Present location and survival have not been established.

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing's complete private transcript of Naples MS XIII.B.29
notes: Kaluza reports using Kölbing's complete private copy in addition to Kölbing's published 1877 variant collation.
The current schema has no working-transcript entity type; manuscript_witness is used provisionally for this nineteenth-century intermediary.
No current holding or digitization was located.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Zur Überlieferung und Quelle des mittelenglischen Gedichtes: Lybeaus Disconus,' Englische Studien 1 (1877): 121-169.

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing's published Naples collation and source study (1877)
notes: The article prints a detailed Naples variant collation against Ritson's Cotton text and discusses analogues; it is not itself a complete continuous Naples transcript.

rights.basis: First published in 1877 and in the public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1877 article and collation

bibliographic.citation: John W. Hales and Frederick J. Furnivall, eds., Bishop Percy's Folio Manuscript: Ballads and Romances, vol. 2 (London: N. Trubner, 1867-1868), 404-499.
bibliographic.title: Bishop Percy's Folio Manuscript: Ballads and Romances

entities.label: Hales and Furnivall's Bishop Percy's Folio Manuscript, volume 2 (1867-1868)
notes: The main Percy transcript occupies printed pp. 415-497 and ends at numbered line 2241; pp. 497-499 add terminal passages from Lincoln's Inn, Ashmole, Cotton, and Lambeth.
Kaluza used this publication for P after checking its first 200 lines directly against the manuscript.

rights.basis: First published in 1867-1868 and in the public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1867-1868 edition and transcription

access.entityLabel: Salisbury and Weldon's Libeaus Desconus (2013)

rights.basis: METS states that all METS texts are copyrighted, digital editions are provided for individual use, and permission is required to reprint or make more extensive use; only the separately distributed XML is identified as CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
rights.component: Modern digital edition, introduction, notes, and site presentation

access.entityLabel: Salisbury and Weldon's Libeaus Desconus (2013)

localCopies.label: Complete official METS downloadable edition PDF
notes: The provider PDF is retained byte-for-byte. pdfinfo and pdfseparate expose and separate all 212 pages successfully; qpdf --check completes with recoverable warnings that multiple objects have offset 0, while file(1) misreports eight pages. No repair, normalization, or rewrite was performed.

workPortion.label: Complete Salisbury-Weldon edition of Libeaus Desconus
notes: Complete coverage of the official downloadable edition, not complete manuscript-image coverage; included manuscript figures are separately credited as reproduced by permission.
Public download and individual reading access do not grant open republication rights; METS requires permission for more extensive reuse.
The official provider PDF has recoverable internal offset warnings but yields all 212 pages; the cached bytes remain unmodified for fixity and provenance.

rights.basis: The official PDF is publicly downloadable, but METS states that its texts remain copyrighted and are for individual use; more extensive reuse requires permission. The XML-only CC BY-NC-SA statement is not extended to this PDF, and included figures carry separate permission credits.
rights.component: Official downloadable 212-page PDF, including modern editorial text and separately credited manuscript figures

bibliographic.citation: Eve Salisbury and James Weldon, eds., Libeaus Desconus (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2013).
bibliographic.title: Libeaus Desconus

entities.label: Salisbury and Weldon's Libeaus Desconus (2013)
notes: A modern downstream edition presenting Lambeth and Naples as separate texts; it is evidence for the work and witnesses, not an ancestor of CME00144.

rights.basis: METS states that all METS texts are copyrighted, digital editions are provided for individual use, and permission is required to reprint or make more extensive use; only the separately distributed XML is identified as CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
rights.component: Modern digital edition, introduction, notes, and site presentation

rights.basis: The official PDF is publicly downloadable, but METS states that its texts remain copyrighted and are for individual use; more extensive reuse requires permission. The XML-only CC BY-NC-SA statement is not extended to this PDF, and included figures carry separate permission credits.
rights.component: Official downloadable 212-page PDF, including modern editorial text and separately credited manuscript figures

entities.label: Lincoln's Inn, Hale MS 150 (I)
notes: Very imperfect witness, 1,107 lines in DIMEV.
Current physical sequence is ff. 1r-10v (provider images 9-28). Kaluza and DIMEV's legacy sequence ff. 4-9, f. 1, ff. 10-12v maps to current ff. 1-6, f. 7, ff. 8-10v.
Composite romance manuscript; the institutional viewer broadly describes a fourteenth-century hand, while modern item-level scholarship gives more differentiated dating.

rights.basis: The medieval wording predates modern copyright; this does not extend to manuscript photography or provider metadata.
rights.component: Underlying medieval textual content

entities.label: Bodleian Libraries, MS Ashmole 61 (A)
notes: Libeaus Desconus occupies ff. 38v-59v and has 2,250 lines in DIMEV.
Copied in England, perhaps Leicester, in the late fifteenth or early sixteenth century by the Rate or Rathe scribe.

rights.basis: The medieval wording predates modern copyright; this does not extend to manuscript photography or provider metadata.
rights.component: Underlying medieval textual content

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 306 (L)
notes: Libeaus Desconus occupies ff. 73r-107r and has 2,204 lines in DIMEV.
Composite manuscript of fifteenth- and sixteenth-century fascicles; this item is dated about 1465 in the current catalog.

rights.basis: The medieval wording predates modern copyright; this does not extend to manuscript photography or provider metadata.
rights.component: Underlying medieval textual content

next_steps: Search Kaluza, Rhode, and Kölbing archival papers, library correspondence, and edition-production records.
If found, record exact holdings and determine whether the documents are separable copies or a combined collation.
Preserve the current indirect lineage until physical or catalog evidence supports a more exact model.
openQuestions.question: Do August Rhode's four witness copies or Eugen Kölbing's complete Naples transcript survive in an identifiable archive?

next_steps: Submit separate BL digitisation requests for Cotton ff. 42vb-57rb and Add MS 27879 modern ff. 156v-171r, including shared boundary surfaces.
Request color photography and written terms for private retention, automated analysis, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, and commercial print-on-demand use.
Keep any historical selected images classified as partial unless the full requested work sequence is delivered.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, retainable work images be obtained for Cotton Caligula A II and Add MS 27879 when neither current BL record exposes a complete public facsimile?

next_steps: Ask the BNN manuscripts service to confirm the current shelfmark, catalog description, and whether complete unpublished digital files exist.
Request the complete physical object, preserving covers, spine, flyleaves, blanks, and all leaves, and map pp. 87a-113b only after receipt.
Obtain written terms for retention, analysis, quotation, redistribution, electronic publication, and commercial use.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current BNN catalog identifier, digitization status, and reusable image route for Naples MS XIII.B.29?

next_steps: Consult the most recent full Lincoln's Inn manuscript catalog and codicological scholarship.
Ask the library to confirm the refoliation or rebinding history underlying the legacy I sequence.
Do not replace current image mapping with the legacy order; preserve both as explicitly labeled locator systems.
openQuestions.question: How should the institutional viewer's broad fourteenth-century description of Hale MS 150 be reconciled with modern item-level dating, and can the current-to-legacy foliation crosswalk be confirmed from a full codicological catalog?

next_steps: Review current textual scholarship across all six witnesses rather than treating shorter or longer readings as automatically authorial.
Distinguish analogy with Renaut de Bage's Li Biaus Descouneus from proof of direct derivation.
Keep Thomas Chestre's name disputed unless new external authorship evidence appears.
openQuestions.question: What confidence should attach to proposed CL and ANP recensions, a lost French source, and the historical attribution to Thomas Chestre?

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's 2,230-line Naples count with Kaluza's historical 2,275 and METS numbering, separating post-explicit moral lines and the Latin colophon.
Align stanza lines rather than comparing rhyme-letter labels in isolation.
Retain the computationally secure 2,232-line extent only for Kaluza's critical text and CME00144's direct poem children.
openQuestions.question: How should differing Naples line counts and the alternative normalized tail-rhyme patterns in DIMEV and the Middle English Romance database be reconciled?

next_steps: Seek a repository-owner statement identifying the license scope for imported corpus XML.
Preserve U-M's NoC-US statement and the public-domain status of Kaluza's edition as separate layer-specific evidence.
Do not infer rights in manuscript photography from rights in medieval words or nineteenth-century editions.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's Unlicense unambiguously cover the imported U-M transcription and markup as well as repository-authored code?

reviewNotes: All nineteen local cache paths are recorded with byte size, SHA-256, source URL, coverage, retrieval date, and work-portion mapping where applicable.

Direct and mediated consultation are modeled separately: Kaluza's I/A/L/C recollation is not collapsed into Rhode's copies, the complete Kölbing transcript is not conflated with the 1877 article, and the first-200-line Percy check is not generalized to the full witness.
A source URL and locator audit was completed on 2026-07-13; remaining provider-access gaps are explicit open questions rather than inferred absences.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a University of Michigan encoding of Max Kaluza's 1890 eclectic critical edition of Libeaus Desconus. Kaluza constructed a 2,232-line text from six manuscripts through unequal routes: August Rhode's working copies plus Kaluza's complete recollation for Lincoln's Inn, Ashmole, Lambeth, and Cotton; Eugen Kölbing's published Naples collation and an unlocated complete private Naples transcript; and Hales and Furnivall's Percy Folio edition, checked directly by Kaluza against the first 200 Percy lines. The XML embeds alternate-witness passages in notes, so its 2,533 descendant L elements must not be mistaken for the edition's or work's line extent. Complete public-image bundles are cached for Ashmole 61, Lambeth 306, and Lincoln's Inn Hale 150; no complete public work facsimile was found for the two British Library witnesses or Naples XIII.B.29.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed circa 1325-1350; Cotton Caligula A II and Lincoln's Inn Hale 150 are dated circa 1400 in the METS introduction, with conflicting institutional and database dates preserved; Kaluza's critical edition was published in 1890
catalogSummary.title: Libeaus Desconus

catalogingSubject.label: Libeaus Desconus
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English Arthurian tail-rhyme romance conventionally titled Libeaus Desconus. It includes the abstract work represented by six textually divergent witnesses but keeps individual manuscript copies, proposed recensions, nineteenth- and twenty-first-century editions, working transcripts, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and facsimiles as distinct layers. The extent of 186 stanzas and 2,232 lines describes Max Kaluza's reconstructed 1890 critical text as encoded in CME00144, not an invariant archetypal extent and not any one manuscript's line count. Kaluza's German and Latin apparatus and 301 embedded alternate-witness lines are edition-level material and do not make the medieval work multilingual or 2,533 lines long.

contentParts.label: Libeaus Desconus
contentParts.summary: The complete 186-stanza critical reading text follows the Fair Unknown hero from concealed upbringing and arrival at Arthur's court through knightly quests, the disenchantment of the Lady of Synadoun, recognition, and marriage.

assertion.notes: The Middle English Romance database gives circa 1325-1350, while the METS introduction uses circa 1350; the broader range is retained rather than presenting a single year as exact.

This is a joint qualified attestation window because the evidence does not establish which witness is earlier. METS dates both item texts circa 1400; its Cotton date conflicts with the current BL component record's second-half-fifteenth-century date and the Middle English Romance database's circa 1446-1460 date, while the Lincoln viewer gives a broader fourteenth-century description and leaves composite/refoliation history unresolved. These source-specific claims remain separate in lineage rather than being silently reconciled.

dateStatements.display: Cotton Caligula A II and Lincoln's Inn Hale MS 150 each dated circa 1400 in the METS introduction

extent.description: Kaluza's represented critical reading text comprises 186 numbered twelve-line stanzas and 2,232 direct verse lines in the sole BODY/DIV1 poem division. The count excludes the title page, headings, milestones, 2,272 NOTE elements, and the 42 LG/301 L elements embedded inside apparatus notes. Witness-specific extents range from the fragmentary 1,107-line Lincoln text to the 2,250-line Ashmole text and must not be summed or normalized to this critical-edition total.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse organized primarily in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas.

assertion.notes: The hero is Gawain's son, receives his name and quests at Arthur's court, and operates within Arthurian romance conventions.
The label identifies a narrative tradition and group of analogues, not proof of direct descent from a specific extant French text.
German editorial commentary, Latin abbreviations and colophon material, and modern English introductions belong to editions or witnesses rather than making the abstract romance multilingual.

notes: Abstract work, proposed recension, six manuscript witnesses, nineteenth-century working transcripts and collations, scholarly editions, digital encoding, repository artifact, and facsimiles are modeled as distinct layers.
The XML's TEXT/@LANG value includes Middle English and German for the edition object, but the cataloged medieval work language is Middle English only.
The edition-specific 186-stanza/2,232-line extent is computationally reproducible from direct BODY/DIV1 children and independently reported for Kaluza by DIMEV.
The six witness line counts are not additive measures of work extent: A 2,250; C 2,130; P 2,241; L 2,204; I 1,107 and very imperfect; N 2,230 in current DIMEV.
Narrative analogues are related works, not silently promoted to direct sources; the lost French antecedent remains a hypothesis rather than an entity.

next_steps: Review current authorship scholarship and the transmission history of the attribution.
Compare linguistic and stylistic arguments with securely attributable texts without treating resemblance as external proof.
Retain Anonymous as the preferred responsibility and Thomas Chestre as disputed until stronger evidence appears.
openQuestions.question: Is there any external evidence sufficient to establish or decisively reject the historical attribution of Libeaus Desconus to Thomas Chestre?

next_steps: Compare the full Middle English witness tradition with Li Biaus Descouneus and other Fair Unknown analogues.
Separate shared motifs, common ancestry, direct translation, and independent adaptation.
Do not model the extant French romance or a hypothetical lost text as a confirmed direct source without demonstrable textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does Libeaus Desconus derive directly from an identifiable French text, a lost French version, or a looser Fair Unknown narrative tradition?

next_steps: Collate all six witnesses at shared and divergent passages using complete images where available.
Test proposed shared errors and interpolations independently of Kaluza's C/z/P2 grouping.
Preserve recensional labels as scholarly hypotheses until the evidence supports a stable stemmatic model.
openQuestions.question: How securely do proposed CL and ANP recensions represent historical textual branches rather than editorial groupings, and can either shorter or longer form be treated as authorial?

next_steps: Review current metrical, lexical, dialectal, and intertextual dating arguments.
Distinguish authorial language reconstructed across witnesses from individual scribal dialects and copy dates.
Retain the current range and regional confidence until newer consensus evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Can the circa 1325-1350 date and South-East Midlands composition localization be narrowed without conflating work origin with later witness dialects?

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's 2,230-line Naples count with Kaluza's historical 2,275 and METS numbering, separating post-explicit moral lines and the Latin colophon.
Align stanza positions and rhyme sounds before interpreting different letter strings as different formal analyses.
Keep 2,232 lines and 186 stanzas explicitly scoped to Kaluza's critical reading text and CME00144's direct poem elements.
openQuestions.question: How should witness-specific line-count discrepancies, especially Naples, and the two published normalized rhyme-pattern strings be reconciled?

assertion.notes: The database assigns South-East Midlands. Witness dialects, including the Naples copy's southwestern associations, are separate evidence layers and do not relocate the abstract work automatically.
No medieval author is securely identified. Later editors, collators, and transcribers remain separate responsibilities.
The historical attribution is retained for retrieval, not accepted as established authorship.

notes: Kaluza reconstructed a reading text from six witnesses, supplied conjectural metrical readings, and normalized several scribal features.

subjects.label: Quest, knightly testing, combat, and tournament
King Arthur, Gawain, and the Round Table

scope.description: The enchanted Lady of Synadoun appears as a dragon and is restored through the hero's kiss.

subjects.label: Travel through forests, courts, cities, gardens, and other secular spaces

summaries.value: Gingelein, the illegitimate son of Gawain, grows up ignorant of his name and lineage. After taking armor and arriving at Arthur's court, he receives the name Libeaus Desconus, the Fair Unknown, and undertakes a series of quests that test his prowess against knights, giants, and magical adversaries. His principal adventure takes him to Synadoun, where a lady transformed into a dragon is freed by his kiss. The romance concludes with recognition, reward, and marriage, joining Arthurian chivalry to the Fair Unknown pattern of concealed identity and earned social standing.

Six witnesses preserve substantially divergent forms and lengths: Lincoln's Inn Hale 150, Bodleian Ashmole 61, Lambeth 306, Cotton Caligula A II, the Percy Folio Add 27879, and Naples XIII.B.29. This record catalogs their shared abstract romance without accepting Kaluza's eclectic reconstruction, Mills's proposed CL/ANP recensions, or the 2013 Lambeth/Naples presentation as the only possible work boundary.

CME00144 encodes Kaluza's 1890 edition as one poem division with 186 direct LG elements and 2,232 direct L elements. Forty-two additional LG and 301 additional L elements are nested inside NOTE apparatus as alternate-witness material. The direct-child counts define this record's edition-specific extent; descendant-wide counts are not work extent. Kaluza's German notes, Latin phrases, page concordances, and title matter remain edition or encoding features rather than work-level language, form, or content parts.

After taking armor and arriving at Arthur's court, he receives the name Libeaus Desconus, the Fair Unknown, and undertakes a series of quests that test his prowess against knights, giants, and magical adversaries. His principal adventure takes him to Synadoun, where a lady transformed into a dragon is freed by his kiss. The romance concludes with recognition, reward, and marriage, joining Arthurian chivalry to the Fair Unknown pattern of concealed identity and earned social standing.
